title: Oesophageal Cancer
oneLiner: Malignancy of oesophagus (adenocarcinoma distal / squamous proximal) – presents late, 5‑year survival ~15–20%, needs urgent dysphagia management and staging.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway obstruction** (tumour invasion / extrinsic compression)
  - *Single finding:* Stridor, hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy) or acute dyspnoea – call anaesthetics / ENT immediately for airway assessment ± stenting.
- **Oesophageal perforation** (spontaneous / post‑procedure)
  - *Single finding:* Sudden severe chest/epigastric pain with surgical emphysema (palpable crackling in neck/chest) – NBM, IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics, urgent CT chest ± contrast swallow, surgical referral.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (progressive):**
  - Dysphagia (solids → liquids → saliva) – **most common**
  - Weight loss / anorexia (>10% in 6 months is red flag)
  - Odynophagia (pain on swallowing)
  - Regurgitation of food / saliva, vomiting
  - Hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Cachexia, muscle wasting
  - Supraclavicular (Virchow’s) node – firm, fixed, left side
  - Hepatomegaly / ascites (liver metastases)
  - Signs of pleural effusion (dullness, reduced breath sounds)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **GORD** – heartburn, intermittent dysphagia, responds to PPI trial; less weight loss
- **Benign oesophageal stricture** – usually past history of GORD or caustic ingestion; no systemic symptoms
- **Achalasia** – regurgitation of undigested food, chest pain, “bird‑beak” on barium swallow
- **Lung cancer** – cough, haemoptysis, lobar collapse on imaging
- **Gastric cardia cancer** – similar presentation; OGD + biopsy differentiates
- **Splitter:** *PPI trial (2 weeks) → if dysphagia resolves completely, likely GORD not cancer. But never delay endoscopy.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Swallow screen (if unable to handle saliva → NBM immediately)
  - Nutritional assessment (MUST score – mandatory on admission)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia of chronic disease / iron deficiency)
  - U&E (dehydration, renal function for chemo)
  - LFTs / albumin (nutritional status, liver metastases)
  - Calcium (hypercalcaemia from squamous cell)
  - Coagulation screen (if liver involvement / planned intervention)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) + biopsy – gold standard
  - **Second-line:** Barium swallow (if OGD incomplete or can’t scope)
  - **Staging:** CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast
  - If non‑metastatic → PET‑CT (excludes occult mets) + endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) for T/N stage
- **Special tests:**
  - Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) for depth of wall invasion and nodal involvement
  - Bronchoscopy if proximal tumour (rule out airway invasion)
- **Never-miss test:** **OGD with biopsy** – never start treatment without histological confirmation.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway compromise → call anaesthetics / ENT for urgent airway assessment ± stent
  - Suspected perforation → NBM, IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics (co‑amoxiclav 1.2g TDS or piperacillin‑tazobactam if severe), IV fluids, urgent CT, surgical consult
  - Significant dysphagia → NBM, IV fluids (Hartmann’s 1L over 8 hours – titrate to hydration), SALT review
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Nutritional support: dietitian referral, consider NG tube if safe (or nasojejunal/jejunostomy if complete obstruction)
  - Pain control: paracetamol 1g QDS + weak opioid (e.g. codeine 30–60mg QDS) if tolerating sips; avoid long‑acting opioids until swallowing assessed
  - PPI (omeprazole 20mg PO/IV BD) for symptom relief and reflux control
  - Treat anaemia: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) if ferritin low and Hb <100 g/L – check haematinics first
  - Involve oncology team for staging + MDT discussion
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Curative intent:** neoadjuvant chemo‑radiotherapy (e.g. FLOT regimen for adenocarcinoma) then surgery (Ivor‑Lewis oesophagectomy) OR definitive chemo‑radiotherapy (for squamous / T2N0)
  - **Palliative:** oesophageal stent (self‑expanding metal stent) for dysphagia, external beam radiotherapy, brachytherapy, palliative chemotherapy (e.g. cisplatin + fluorouracil), best supportive care
- **Default Orders (if admitted for dysphagia):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at 1–1.5 L/24h, adjust for dehydration/weight
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (check weight <120kg and eGFR >30 – if obese or renal impairment use [check local policy])
  - **Diet/NBM:** if dysphagia to saliva → NBM; if partial dysphagia → modified consistency (dietitian/SALT)
  - **Observations:** NEWS 4–6 hourly; if weight loss or frailty consider lying + standing BP
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **PPI:** Omeprazole 20mg PO/IV BD (or lansoprazole 30mg OD) – for symptom control and GORD
- **Antiemetic:** Ondansetron 4–8mg IV/PO PRN (avoid metoclopramide if complete obstruction – can cause pain)
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g QDS (max 4g/day)
  - Codeine 30–60mg QDS (only if swallowing safe – constipating)
  - Morphine 2.5–5mg SC PRN for acute pain – reassess before repeat; start low in cachectic patients
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if eGFR >30; if <30 use unfractionated heparin [check local policy])
- **Key contraindication:** Avoid NSAIDs (risk of bleeding / perforation)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce for eGFR <30 (e.g. 20mg OD or UFH)
  - Opioids: reduced dose in renal impairment (avoid morphine if eGFR <30 – use fentanyl)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Opioid overdose in frail, dehydrated patient with reduced oral intake** – start with short‑acting and low dose (e.g. morphine 2.5mg SC), reassess before repeat. Never prescribe modified‑release until swallowing stable.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Aspiration pneumonia (from dysphagia/regurgitation)
  - Oesophageal perforation (post‑biopsy/stent/chemo‑radiotherapy)
  - Stent migration / tumour overgrowth
  - Tracheo‑oesophageal fistula (coughing after swallowing, recurrent pneumonia)
  - Aorto‑oesophageal fistula (sentinel haematemesis → catastrophic bleed)
- **Chronic:**
  - Malnutrition / cachexia / sarcopenia
  - Recurrence (often within 2 years)
  - Stricture after radiotherapy
  - Post‑surgical: anastomotic leak, dumping syndrome, reflux, vagal nerve injury
sections.Prognosis:
- **Overall 5‑year survival ~15–20%** – most present with advanced disease.
- **Localised disease (T1‑3N0M0):** 5‑year survival 30–40% with neoadjuvant therapy + surgery.
- **Metastatic:** median survival 9–12 months (palliative chemo ± best supportive care).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Calculate MUST score** on every admission – refer to dietitian if score ≥2.
2. **Arrange urgent (2‑week wait) OGD** in any patient >55 with dysphagia or unexplained weight loss.
3. **Involve SALT** before allowing any oral intake in confirmed dysphagia – objective swallow assessment prevents aspiration.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Delaying OGD** – do not start treatment (stent/radiotherapy) without biopsy confirmation.
2. **Prescribing prokinetics (metoclopramide, domperidone)** in complete obstruction – causes severe pain and risk of perforation.
3. **Assuming “just acid reflux”** in older patient with weight loss – always arrange endoscopy even if PPI trial helps.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Unable to swallow own saliva (complete obstruction)
  - New hoarseness / stridor / respiratory distress
  - Haematemesis or melaena
  - Severe chest pain or fever after procedure (possible perforation)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Outpatient oncology / upper GI MDT within 2 weeks
  - Dietitian and SALT if dysphagia persists
  - Colonoscopy if iron deficiency anaemia and no obvious source (consider synchronous gastric cancer)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Stridor / impending airway loss
  - Haemodynamic instability from bleeding
- **Call senior / surgical reg if:**
  - Suspected oesophageal perforation (immediate CT + surgical referral)
  - New hoarseness or stridor (ENT review for airway assessment)
  - Haematemesis >100ml or persistent melaena (urgent OGD / interventional radiology)

rawMarkdown:
## Oesophageal Cancer
**One-liner:** Malignancy of oesophagus (adenocarcinoma distal / squamous proximal) – presents late, 5‑year survival ~15–20%, needs urgent dysphagia management and staging.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway obstruction** (tumour invasion / extrinsic compression)
  - *Single finding:* Stridor, hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy) or acute dyspnoea – call anaesthetics / ENT immediately for airway assessment ± stenting.
- **Oesophageal perforation** (spontaneous / post‑procedure)
  - *Single finding:* Sudden severe chest/epigastric pain with surgical emphysema (palpable crackling in neck/chest) – NBM, IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics, urgent CT chest ± contrast swallow, surgical referral.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (progressive):**
  - Dysphagia (solids → liquids → saliva) – **most common**
  - Weight loss / anorexia (>10% in 6 months is red flag)
  - Odynophagia (pain on swallowing)
  - Regurgitation of food / saliva, vomiting
  - Hoarseness (recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Cachexia, muscle wasting
  - Supraclavicular (Virchow’s) node – firm, fixed, left side
  - Hepatomegaly / ascites (liver metastases)
  - Signs of pleural effusion (dullness, reduced breath sounds)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **GORD** – heartburn, intermittent dysphagia, responds to PPI trial; less weight loss
- **Benign oesophageal stricture** – usually past history of GORD or caustic ingestion; no systemic symptoms
- **Achalasia** – regurgitation of undigested food, chest pain, “bird‑beak” on barium swallow
- **Lung cancer** – cough, haemoptysis, lobar collapse on imaging
- **Gastric cardia cancer** – similar presentation; OGD + biopsy differentiates
- **Splitter:** *PPI trial (2 weeks) → if dysphagia resolves completely, likely GORD not cancer. But never delay endoscopy.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Swallow screen (if unable to handle saliva → NBM immediately)
  - Nutritional assessment (MUST score – mandatory on admission)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia of chronic disease / iron deficiency)
  - U&E (dehydration, renal function for chemo)
  - LFTs / albumin (nutritional status, liver metastases)
  - Calcium (hypercalcaemia from squamous cell)
  - Coagulation screen (if liver involvement / planned intervention)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) + biopsy – gold standard
  - **Second-line:** Barium swallow (if OGD incomplete or can’t scope)
  - **Staging:** CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast
  - If non‑metastatic → PET‑CT (excludes occult mets) + endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) for T/N stage
- **Special tests:**
  - Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) for depth of wall invasion and nodal involvement
  - Bronchoscopy if proximal tumour (rule out airway invasion)
- **Never-miss test:** **OGD with biopsy** – never start treatment without histological confirmation.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Airway compromise → call anaesthetics / ENT for urgent airway assessment ± stent
  - Suspected perforation → NBM, IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics (co‑amoxiclav 1.2g TDS or piperacillin‑tazobactam if severe), IV fluids, urgent CT, surgical consult
  - Significant dysphagia → NBM, IV fluids (Hartmann’s 1L over 8 hours – titrate to hydration), SALT review
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Nutritional support: dietitian referral, consider NG tube if safe (or nasojejunal/jejunostomy if complete obstruction)
  - Pain control: paracetamol 1g QDS + weak opioid (e.g. codeine 30–60mg QDS) if tolerating sips; avoid long‑acting opioids until swallowing assessed
  - PPI (omeprazole 20mg PO/IV BD) for symptom relief and reflux control
  - Treat anaemia: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) if ferritin low and Hb <100 g/L – check haematinics first
  - Involve oncology team for staging + MDT discussion
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Curative intent:** neoadjuvant chemo‑radiotherapy (e.g. FLOT regimen for adenocarcinoma) then surgery (Ivor‑Lewis oesophagectomy) OR definitive chemo‑radiotherapy (for squamous / T2N0)
  - **Palliative:** oesophageal stent (self‑expanding metal stent) for dysphagia, external beam radiotherapy, brachytherapy, palliative chemotherapy (e.g. cisplatin + fluorouracil), best supportive care
- **Default Orders (if admitted for dysphagia):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at 1–1.5 L/24h, adjust for dehydration/weight
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (check weight <120kg and eGFR >30 – if obese or renal impairment use [check local policy])
  - **Diet/NBM:** if dysphagia to saliva → NBM; if partial dysphagia → modified consistency (dietitian/SALT)
  - **Observations:** NEWS 4–6 hourly; if weight loss or frailty consider lying + standing BP

### Drugs & Doses
- **PPI:** Omeprazole 20mg PO/IV BD (or lansoprazole 30mg OD) – for symptom control and GORD
- **Antiemetic:** Ondansetron 4–8mg IV/PO PRN (avoid metoclopramide if complete obstruction – can cause pain)
- **Analgesia:**
  - Paracetamol 1g QDS (max 4g/day)
  - Codeine 30–60mg QDS (only if swallowing safe – constipating)
  - Morphine 2.5–5mg SC PRN for acute pain – reassess before repeat; start low in cachectic patients
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if eGFR >30; if <30 use unfractionated heparin [check local policy])
- **Key contraindication:** Avoid NSAIDs (risk of bleeding / perforation)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: reduce for eGFR <30 (e.g. 20mg OD or UFH)
  - Opioids: reduced dose in renal impairment (avoid morphine if eGFR <30 – use fentanyl)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Opioid overdose in frail, dehydrated patient with reduced oral intake** – start with short‑acting and low dose (e.g. morphine 2.5mg SC), reassess before repeat. Never prescribe modified‑release until swallowing stable.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Aspiration pneumonia (from dysphagia/regurgitation)
  - Oesophageal perforation (post‑biopsy/stent/chemo‑radiotherapy)
  - Stent migration / tumour overgrowth
  - Tracheo‑oesophageal fistula (coughing after swallowing, recurrent pneumonia)
  - Aorto‑oesophageal fistula (sentinel haematemesis → catastrophic bleed)
- **Chronic:**
  - Malnutrition / cachexia / sarcopenia
  - Recurrence (often within 2 years)
  - Stricture after radiotherapy
  - Post‑surgical: anastomotic leak, dumping syndrome, reflux, vagal nerve injury

### Prognosis
- **Overall 5‑year survival ~15–20%** – most present with advanced disease.
- **Localised disease (T1‑3N0M0):** 5‑year survival 30–40% with neoadjuvant therapy + surgery.
- **Metastatic:** median survival 9–12 months (palliative chemo ± best supportive care).

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Calculate MUST score** on every admission – refer to dietitian if score ≥2.
2. **Arrange urgent (2‑week wait) OGD** in any patient >55 with dysphagia or unexplained weight loss.
3. **Involve SALT** before allowing any oral intake in confirmed dysphagia – objective swallow assessment prevents aspiration.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Delaying OGD** – do not start treatment (stent/radiotherapy) without biopsy confirmation.
2. **Prescribing prokinetics (metoclopramide, domperidone)** in complete obstruction – causes severe pain and risk of perforation.
3. **Assuming “just acid reflux”** in older patient with weight loss – always arrange endoscopy even if PPI trial helps.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Unable to swallow own saliva (complete obstruction)
  - New hoarseness / stridor / respiratory distress
  - Haematemesis or melaena
  - Severe chest pain or fever after procedure (possible perforation)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Outpatient oncology / upper GI MDT within 2 weeks
  - Dietitian and SALT if dysphagia persists
  - Colonoscopy if iron deficiency anaemia and no obvious source (consider synchronous gastric cancer)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call crash team / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or rising
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Stridor / impending airway loss
  - Haemodynamic instability from bleeding
- **Call senior / surgical reg if:**
  - Suspected oesophageal perforation (immediate CT + surgical referral)
  - New hoarseness or stridor (ENT review for airway assessment)
  - Haematemesis >100ml or persistent melaena (urgent OGD / interventional radiology)
